steps.title: Read the claim precisely
steps.content: Trollope's portrayal underscores Dr. Proudie's exaggerated sense of his own abilities. The quotation must show Proudie himself overrating his abilities, not the narrator's judgment or someone else's view.

steps.title: Scan for self-flattery
steps.content: Look for language showing Proudie thinking highly of himself. The phrase "as he fondly flattered himself" is a textbook signal of a character's inflated self-image.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'must not...be taken as proved that Dr. Proudie was a man of great mental powers': this is the narrator deflating Proudie, not Proudie overrating himself (misread of whose view is shown). ✗
  • Choice B — 'as he fondly flattered himself, been selected as possessing such gifts...as must be sure to recommend him to a yet higher notice': Proudie himself imagines he has superior gifts destined for promotion — exactly an exaggerated self-assessment. ✓
  • Choice C — 'his high connexions, and the peculiar talents...recommended him to persons in power': the narrator lists external reasons he was noticed; nothing about Proudie's self-view (misread). ✗
  • Choice D — 'sufficient tact to answer the purpose...without making himself troublesome': describes a modest, useful competence, the opposite of an inflated self-image (misread). ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
